content: If $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{b}$ are two vectors such that $\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}=|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|$ then the angle between $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{b}$ is
explanation:
First write the two identities:

$\displaystyle \vec a\cdot\vec b = |\vec a|\,|\vec b|\cos\theta$

$\displaystyle |\vec a\times\vec b| = |\vec a|\,|\vec b|\,|\sin\theta|$

Setting them equal gives

$ |\vec a|\,|\vec b|\cos\theta \;=\; |\vec a|\,|\vec b|\,|\sin\theta| \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \cos\theta = |\sin\theta|. $

So

$ \tan\theta = \pm1 \;\Longrightarrow\; \theta = \frac\pi4\text{ or }\frac{3\pi}4. $

But $\vec a\cdot\vec b=|\vec a\times\vec b|\ge0$ forces $\cos\theta\ge0$, excluding $3\pi/4$.

Answer: $\displaystyle \theta=\frac\pi4$. (Option A)

content: For any vector $\vec{p}$, the value of $\left[2\left\{|\vec{p} \times \hat{\imath}|^2+|\vec{p} \times \hat{\jmath}|^2+|\vec{p} \times \hat{k}|^2\right\}\right]$ is
explanation:
Let $\vec p=(p_x,p_y,p_z)$. Then

$\displaystyle|\vec p\times\hat\imath|^2 = p_y^2+p_z^2$

$\displaystyle|\vec p\times\hat\jmath|^2 = p_x^2+p_z^2$

$\displaystyle|\vec p\times\hat k|^2 = p_x^2+p_y^2$

So

$ |\vec p\times\hat\imath|^2+|\vec p\times\hat\jmath|^2+|\vec p\times\hat k|^2 =2(p_x^2+p_y^2+p_z^2) =2\,|\vec p|^2. $

Multiplying by 2 gives

$ 2\bigl\{\,\cdots\bigr\}=2\cdot2|\vec p|^2=4|\vec p|^2, $

which is Option A.

content: If $|\vec{a}|=2 \sqrt{2}$ and $|\vec{b}|=3$ and angle between $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{b}$ is $\frac{\pi}{4}$. If a parallelogram is constructed with adjacent sides $\vec{p}=2 \vec{a}-3 \vec{b}$ and $\vec{q}=\vec{a}+\vec{b}$ then the product of length of both the diagonals is :
explanation:
First, note the two diagonals of the parallelogram are

• d₁ = p + q = (2a − 3b) + (a + b) = 3a − 2b

• d₂ = p − q = (2a − 3b) − (a + b) = a − 4b

We want |d₁|·|d₂|. Compute step by step:

Compute the dot-products and magnitudes you’ll need:

• |a|² = (2√2)² = 8

• |b|² = 3² = 9

• a·b = |a| |b| cos(π/4) = (2√2)(3)(√2/2) = 6

|d₁|² = |3a − 2b|²

= 9|a|² + 4|b|² − 2·3·2 (a·b)

= 9·8 + 4·9 − 12·6

= 72 + 36 − 72

= 36

⇒ |d₁| = 6

|d₂|² = |a − 4b|²

= |a|² + 16|b|² − 2·1·4 (a·b)

= 8 + 144 − 8·6

= 8 + 144 − 48

= 104

⇒ |d₂| = √104 = 2√26

Product of the lengths

|d₁|·|d₂| = 6 · (2√26) = 12√26

Answer: 12√26 (Option A)

content: Position vector of P and Q are $\hat{\imath}+3 \hat{\jmath}-7 \hat{k}$ and $5 \hat{\imath}-2 \hat{\jmath}+4 \hat{k}$ respectively. Then the cosine of the angle between $\overrightarrow{P Q}$ and y -axis is
explanation:
Let P = (1, 3, −7) and Q = (5, −2, 4). The angle between $\overrightarrow{PQ}$ and the y-axis satisfies

Compute $\overrightarrow{PQ}$:

$$ \overrightarrow{PQ} = Q - P = (5 - 1)\,\hat\imath +(-2 - 3)\,\hat\jmath + (4 - (-7))\,\hat k = 4\,\hat\imath - 5\,\hat\jmath + 11\,\hat k $$

Its magnitude:

$$ \bigl|\overrightarrow{PQ}\bigr| = \sqrt{4^2 + (-5)^2 + 11^2} = \sqrt{16 + 25 + 121} = \sqrt{162} $$

The y-axis direction is $\hat\jmath$. So

$$ \cos\theta = \frac{\overrightarrow{PQ}\cdot\hat\jmath}{|\overrightarrow{PQ}|\;|\hat\jmath|} = \frac{-5}{\sqrt{162}}\,. $$

Answer: $\displaystyle -\frac{5}{\sqrt{162}}$ (Option C).

content: If $\vec{a}, \vec{b}, \vec{c}$ are three vectors such that $a \neq 0$ and $\vec{a} \times \vec{b}=2(\vec{a} \times \vec{c}),|\vec{a}|=|\vec{c}|=1,|\vec{b}|=4$ and $|\vec{b} \times \vec{c}|=\sqrt{15}$ if $\vec{b}-2 \vec{c}=\lambda \vec{a}$ then $\lambda^2$ equals :
explanation:
First, note that from

$$\vec b - 2\vec c = \lambda\,\vec a$$

and $|\vec a|=1$ we get

$$\lambda^2 = |\vec b - 2\vec c|^2 = |\vec b|^2 +4|\vec c|^2 -4\,\vec b\cdot\vec c = 16 +4 -4(\vec b\cdot\vec c) = 20 -4(\vec b\cdot\vec c)\,. $$

Meanwhile the identity

$$|\vec b\times\vec c|^2 + (\vec b\cdot\vec c)^2 = |\vec b|^2\,|\vec c|^2 = 16\cdot1 =16$$

and the given $|\vec b\times\vec c|^2=15$ imply

$$(\vec b\cdot\vec c)^2 =16-15=1 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \vec b\cdot\vec c = \pm1.$$

If $\vec b\cdot\vec c=-1$, then

$$\lambda^2 =20 -4(-1)=24,$$

which isn’t among the options. Hence we take $\vec b\cdot\vec c=+1$, giving

$$\lambda^2 =20 -4\cdot1 =16.$$

Answer: 16.

content: A line $L_1$ passing through the point A with position vector $\vec{a}=4 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}$ is parallel to the vector $\vec{b}=2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}$. The length of the perpendicular drawn from a point P with position vector $\vec{p}=\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}$ to $L_1$ is
explanation:
First form the vector from A to P:

$$\overrightarrow{AP}=\vec p-\vec a=(1,2,3)-(4,2,2)=(-3,0,1)\,. $$

The distance from P to the line through A in direction $\vec b$ is

$$\text{dist}=\frac{\bigl\|\overrightarrow{AP}\times\vec b\bigr\|}{\|\vec b\|}\,. $$

Compute the cross product:

$$\overrightarrow{AP}\times\vec b =\det\begin{pmatrix}\mathbf i&\mathbf j&\mathbf k\\-3&0&1\\2&3&6\end{pmatrix} =(-3,20,-9)\,, $$

so

$$\bigl\|\overrightarrow{AP}\times\vec b\bigr\| =\sqrt{(-3)^2+20^2+(-9)^2}=\sqrt{490}=7\sqrt{10},$$

and

$$\|\vec b\|=\sqrt{2^2+3^2+6^2}=\sqrt{49}=7\,. $$

Therefore

$$\text{dist}=\frac{7\sqrt{10}}{7}=\sqrt{10}\,. $$

Answer: $\sqrt{10}$ (Option D).

content: The magnitude of the projection of the vector $-\hat{\imath}+2 \hat{\jmath}-\hat{k}$ on the z -axis is
explanation:
To project onto the z–axis, use the unit vector $\hat k=(0,0,1)$.

• Compute the scalar projection:

$$v\cdot\hat k =(-1)\cdot0 + 2\cdot0 +(-1)\cdot1 = -1.$$

• The magnitude of that projection is

$$\bigl|\,v\cdot\hat k\bigr| = |-1| = 1.$$

Answer: option C (1).

content: $$ \text { If } \hat{\imath}+\hat{\jmath}-\hat{k} \quad \&~ 2 \hat{\imath}-3 \hat{\jmath}+\hat{k} \text { are adjacent sides of a parallelogram, then length of its diagonals are } $$
explanation:
To find the lengths of the diagonals of the parallelogram with adjacent sides represented by the vectors $$ \vec{a} = \hat{\imath} + \hat{\jmath} - \hat{k} $$ and $$ \vec{b} = 2\hat{\imath} - 3\hat{\jmath} + \hat{k} $$, we need to determine the diagonals using the vector addition and subtraction.

The diagonals of the parallelogram can be given by $$ \vec{a} + \vec{b} $$ and $$ \vec{a} - \vec{b} $$.

First, let's compute $$ \vec{a} + \vec{b} $$:

$$ \vec{a} + \vec{b} = (\hat{\imath} + \hat{\jmath} - \hat{k}) + (2\hat{\imath} - 3\hat{\jmath} + \hat{k}) $$

Combining like terms, we get:

$$ \vec{a} + \vec{b} = (1 + 2)\hat{\imath} + (1 - 3)\hat{\jmath} + (-1 + 1)\hat{k} = 3\hat{\imath} - 2\hat{\jmath} $$

Now, let's find the magnitude of this vector:

$$ |\vec{a} + \vec{b}| = \sqrt{(3)^2 + (-2)^2 + (0)^2} = \sqrt{9 + 4} = \sqrt{13} $$

Next, let's compute $$ \vec{a} - \vec{b} $$:

$$ \vec{a} - \vec{b} = (\hat{\imath} + \hat{\jmath} - \hat{k}) - (2\hat{\imath} - 3\hat{\jmath} + \hat{k}) $$

Combining like terms, we get:

$$ \vec{a} - \vec{b} = (1 - 2)\hat{\imath} + (1 + 3)\hat{\jmath} + (-1 - 1)\hat{k} = -\hat{\imath} + 4\hat{\jmath} - 2\hat{k} $$

Now, let's find the magnitude of this vector:

$$ |\vec{a} - \vec{b}| = \sqrt{(-1)^2 + (4)^2 + (-2)^2} = \sqrt{1 + 16 + 4} = \sqrt{21} $$

Thus, the lengths of the diagonals of the parallelogram are $$ \sqrt{13} $$ and $$ \sqrt{21} $$.

The correct option is:

Option D $$ \sqrt{21}, \quad \sqrt{13} $$

content: Find the value of '$$b$$' such that the scalar product of the vector $$\hat{\imath}+\hat{\jmath}+\hat{k}$$ with the unit vector parallel to the sum of the vectors $$2 \hat{\imath}+4 \hat{\jmath}-5 \hat{k}$$ and $$b \hat{\imath}+2 \hat{\jmath}+3 \hat{k}$$ is unity
explanation:
Let's break down the problem step by step.

1. Finding the Sum of the Vectors

The sum of the two vectors is:

$$(2 \hat{\imath}+4 \hat{\jmath}-5 \hat{k}) + (b \hat{\imath}+2 \hat{\jmath}+3 \hat{k}) = (2+b) \hat{\imath} + 6 \hat{\jmath} - 2 \hat{k}$$

2. Finding the Unit Vector

To find the unit vector parallel to the sum, we need to divide the sum by its magnitude:

Magnitude of the sum: $$||(2+b) \hat{\imath} + 6 \hat{\jmath} - 2 \hat{k}|| = \sqrt{(2+b)^2 + 6^2 + (-2)^2} = \sqrt{b^2 + 4b + 44}$$

Unit vector: $$ \frac{(2+b) \hat{\imath} + 6 \hat{\jmath} - 2 \hat{k}}{\sqrt{b^2 + 4b + 44}}$$

3. Scalar Product

The scalar product (dot product) of the vector $$\hat{\imath}+\hat{\jmath}+\hat{k}$$ with the unit vector is given by:

$$(\hat{\imath}+\hat{\jmath}+\hat{k}) \cdot \frac{(2+b) \hat{\imath} + 6 \hat{\jmath} - 2 \hat{k}}{\sqrt{b^2 + 4b + 44}} = \frac{(2+b) + 6 - 2}{\sqrt{b^2 + 4b + 44}}$$

4. Setting the Scalar Product to Unity

We are given that this scalar product is equal to unity (1):

$$ \frac{(2+b) + 6 - 2}{\sqrt{b^2 + 4b + 44}} = 1$$

5. Solving for 'b'

Simplifying the equation and solving for 'b':

$$ b + 6 = \sqrt{b^2 + 4b + 44}$$

Squaring both sides:

$$b^2 + 12b + 36 = b^2 + 4b + 44$$

$$8b = 8$$

$$b = 1$$

Therefore, the value of 'b' is 1, which corresponds to Option D.

content: Let a, b, c be three vector such that $$a \neq 0$$ and $$\vec{a} \times \vec{b}=2 \vec{a} \times \vec{c},|a|=|c|=1,|b|=4$$ and $$|\vec{b} \times \vec{c}|=\sqrt{15}$$. If $$\vec{b}-2 \vec{c}=\lambda \vec{a}$$ then $$\lambda$$ equals to
$$ \text { The angle between } \hat{\imath}-\hat{\jmath} ~\&~ \hat{\jmath}-\hat{k} \text { is } $$
explanation:
Let's determine the angle between the vectors $$\hat{\imath} - \hat{\jmath}$$ and $$\hat{\jmath} - \hat{k}$$ using the dot product formula.

Define the vectors in component form:

$$\mathbf{u} = \hat{\imath} - \hat{\jmath} = (1, -1, 0)$$

$$\mathbf{v} = \hat{\jmath} - \hat{k} = (0, 1, -1)$$

Compute the dot product of the two vectors:

$$ \mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{v} = (1)(0) + (-1)(1) + (0)(-1) = -1 $$

Calculate the magnitudes (norms) of each vector:

For $$\mathbf{u}$$:

$$ \|\mathbf{u}\| = \sqrt{1^2 + (-1)^2 + 0^2} = \sqrt{2} $$

For $$\mathbf{v}$$:

$$ \|\mathbf{v}\| = \sqrt{0^2 + 1^2 + (-1)^2} = \sqrt{2} $$

Use the dot product formula to find the cosine of the angle $$\theta$$ between the vectors:

$$ \cos \theta = \frac{\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{v}}{\|\mathbf{u}\| \|\mathbf{v}\|} = \frac{-1}{\sqrt{2} \cdot \sqrt{2}} = \frac{-1}{2} $$

Determine the angle $$\theta$$:

$$ \theta = \cos^{-1}\left(-\frac{1}{2}\right) = \frac{2\pi}{3} $$

Thus, the angle between $$\hat{\imath}-\hat{\jmath}$$ and $$\hat{\jmath}-\hat{k}$$ is $$\frac{2\pi}{3}$$, which corresponds to Option B.

content: The vector $$(\vec{r})$$ whose magnitude is $$3 \sqrt{2}$$ units which makes an angle of $$\frac{\pi}{4}$$ and $$\frac{\pi}{2}$$ with $$y$$ and $$z$$- axis respectively is
explanation:
Let's determine the vector $$\vec{r}$$ knowing that its magnitude is $$3 \sqrt{2}$$ and it makes angles of $$\frac{\pi}{4}$$ and $$\frac{\pi}{2}$$ with the $$y$$ and $$z$$-axes respectively.

First, we use the relationship between the direction cosines and the angles they make with the coordinate axes. Let the direction cosines be $$l$$, $$m$$, and $$n$$ corresponding to the $$x$$, $$y$$, and $$z$$-axes respectively. These are given by:

$$ l = \cos \alpha
m = \cos \beta
n = \cos \gamma $$

where $$\alpha, \beta, \gamma$$ are the angles the vector makes with the $$x, y,$$ and $$z$$-axes.

From the given information:

  • $$\beta = \frac{\pi}{4}$$
  • $$\gamma = \frac{\pi}{2}$$

We'll find the direction cosines:

  • $$\cos \beta = \cos \frac{\pi}{4} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$$
  • $$\cos \gamma = \cos \frac{\pi}{2} = 0$$

We utilize the fact that the sum of the squares of the direction cosines must be 1:

$$ l^2 + m^2 + n^2 = 1 $$

Plugging in the known values:

$$ l^2 + \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^2 + 0^2 = 1 $$

$$ l^2 + \frac{1}{2} = 1 $$

Solving for $$l^2$$:

$$ l^2 = 1 - \frac{1}{2} = \frac{1}{2} $$

$$ l = \pm \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} $$

Now, the vector $$\vec{r}$$ can be expressed using the direction cosines and the magnitude:

$$ \vec{r} = r \cdot (l \hat{\imath} + m \hat{\jmath} + n \hat{k}) $$

With the magnitude $$r = 3 \sqrt{2}$$, $$ l = \pm \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, m = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, n = 0 $$:

The vector component values:

$$ \vec{r} = 3 \sqrt{2} \left( \pm \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \hat{\imath} + \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \hat{\jmath} + 0 \hat{k} \right) $$

Simplifying:

$$ \vec{r} = \pm 3 \hat{\imath} + 3 \hat{\jmath} $$

Therefore, the correct option is:

Option D
$$\pm 3 \hat{\imath} + 3 \hat{\jmath}$$

content: $$ \text { If }|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|^2+|\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}|^2=144 ~\&~|\vec{a}|=4 \text { then }|\vec{b}|= $$
explanation:
To solve for $$|\vec{b}|$$, we use the given equation:

$$|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|^2 + |\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}|^2 = 144$$

and the given magnitude $$|\vec{a}| = 4$$.

Recall the identities of the dot product and cross product for vectors $$\vec{a}$$ and $$\vec{b}$$:

  1. The magnitude of the dot product is given by:

$$|\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}| = |\vec{a}||\vec{b}| \cos \theta$$

  1. The magnitude of the cross product is given by:

$$|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}| = |\vec{a}||\vec{b}| \sin \theta$$.

Substitute these identities into the given equation:

$$\left(|\vec{a}||\vec{b}| \sin \theta\right)^2 + \left(|\vec{a}||\vec{b}| \cos \theta\right)^2 = 144$$.

$$\left(4|\vec{b}| \sin \theta\right)^2 + \left(4|\vec{b}| \cos \theta\right)^2 = 144$$.

Factor out $$|\vec{b}|$$:

$$16|\vec{b}|^2 (\sin^2 \theta + \cos^2 \theta) = 144$$.

Since $$\sin^2 \theta + \cos^2 \theta = 1$$, we have:

$$16|\vec{b}|^2 = 144$$.

Solve for $$|\vec{b}|^2$$:

$$|\vec{b}|^2 = \frac{144}{16}$$

$$|\vec{b}|^2 = 9$$.

Take the square root of both sides:

$$|\vec{b}| = 3$$.

Therefore, the correct option is:

Option A = 3

content: The angle between the vectors $$\mathbf{a}=\hat{\mathbf{i}}+2 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+2 \hat{\mathbf{k}}$$ and $$\mathbf{b}=\hat{\mathbf{i}}+2 \hat{\mathbf{j}}-2 \hat{\mathbf{k}}$$ is
explanation:
We have, $$\mathbf{a}=\hat{\mathbf{i}}+2 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+2 \hat{\mathbf{k}}$$ and $$\mathbf{b}=\hat{\mathbf{i}}+2 \hat{\mathbf{j}}-2 \hat{\mathbf{k}}$$

Clearly, $$|\mathrm{a}|=\sqrt{1+4+4}=\sqrt{9}=3$$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \text { and } & |\mathbf{b}|=\sqrt{1+4+4}=\sqrt{9}=3 \\ \because & \mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b}=|a||b| \cos \theta \\ \Rightarrow & \cos \theta=\frac{1 \cdot 1+2 \cdot 2+2 \cdot(-2)}{3 \times 3} \\ \Rightarrow & \cos \theta=\frac{1+4-4}{9} \Rightarrow \cos \theta=\frac{1}{9} \\ \therefore & \quad \theta=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{9}\right) \end{array}$$

content: If the vectors $$\mathbf{a}=2 \hat{\mathbf{i}}-3 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+4 \hat{\mathbf{k}} ; \mathbf{b}=\hat{\mathbf{i}}+2 \hat{\mathbf{j}}-\hat{\mathbf{k}}$$ and $$\mathbf{c}=m \hat{\mathbf{i}}-\hat{\mathbf{j}}+2 \hat{\mathbf{k}}$$ are coplanar, then the value of $$m$$ is
explanation:
Since, vectors a, b and c are coplanar

$$\therefore[\mathbf{a ~ b ~ c}]=0 \Rightarrow a \cdot(b \times c)=0$$

$$\begin{aligned} \text { Now, } \mathbf{b} \times \mathbf{c} & =\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{\mathbf{i}} & \hat{\mathbf{j}} & \hat{\mathbf{k}} \\ 1 & 2 & -1 \\ m & -1 & 2 \end{array}\right| \\ & =\hat{\mathbf{i}}(4-1)-\hat{\mathbf{j}}(2+m)+\hat{\mathbf{k}}(-1-2 m) \\ & =3 \hat{\mathbf{i}}-(2+m) \hat{\mathbf{j}}-(1+2 m) \hat{\mathbf{k}} \end{aligned}$$

and

$$\begin{aligned} & \mathbf{a} \cdot(\mathbf{b} \times \mathbf{c})= (2 \hat{\mathbf{i}}-3 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+4 \hat{\mathbf{k}}) \cdot(3 \hat{\mathbf{i}}-(2+m) \hat{\mathbf{j}}-(1+2 m) \hat{\mathbf{k}}) \\ & =2(3)+3(2+m)-4(1+2 m) \\ & \quad=6+6+3 m-4-8 m=8-5 m \\ \therefore& \quad \mathbf{a} \cdot(\mathbf{b} \times \mathbf{c})=0 \\ & \therefore \quad 8-5 m=0 \Rightarrow m=\frac{8}{5} \end{aligned}$$

content: $$\mathbf{a}=2 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+\hat{\mathbf{j}}-\hat{\mathbf{k}}, \mathbf{b}=\hat{\mathbf{i}}-\hat{\mathbf{j}}$$ and $$\mathbf{c}=5 \hat{\mathbf{i}}-\hat{\mathbf{j}}+\hat{\mathbf{k}}$$, then unit vector parallel to $$\mathbf{a}+\mathbf{b}-\mathbf{c}$$ but in opposite direction is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Given, } \mathbf{a}=2 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+\hat{\mathbf{j}}-\hat{\mathbf{k}}, \mathbf{b}=\hat{\mathbf{i}}-\hat{\mathbf{j}} \text { and } \mathbf{c}=5 \hat{\mathbf{i}}-\hat{\mathbf{j}}+\hat{\mathbf{k}} \\ & \begin{aligned} \therefore \mathbf{a}+\mathbf{b}-\mathbf{c} & =(2+1-5) \hat{\mathbf{i}}+(1-1+1) \hat{\mathbf{j}}+(-1+0-1) \hat{\mathbf{k}} \\ & =-2 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+\hat{\mathbf{j}}-2 \hat{\mathbf{k}}=-(2 \hat{\mathbf{i}}-\hat{\mathbf{j}}+2 \hat{\mathbf{k}}) \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

Now, the unit vector in the direction of $$\mathbf{a}+\mathbf{b}-\mathbf{c}$$ be

$$\frac{-(2 \hat{\mathbf{i}}-\hat{\mathbf{j}}+2 \hat{\mathbf{k}})}{\sqrt{2^2+(-1)^2+2^2}}=-\frac{1}{3}(2 \hat{\mathbf{i}}-\hat{\mathbf{j}}+2 \hat{\mathbf{k}})$$

$$\therefore$$ The required unit vector be $$\frac{1}{3}(2 \hat{\mathbf{i}}-\hat{\mathbf{j}}+2 \hat{\mathbf{k}})$$.

content: The scalar components of a unit vector which is perpendicular to each of the vectors $$\hat{\imath}+2 \hat{\jmath}-\hat{k}$$ and $$3 \hat{\imath}-\hat{\jmath}+2 \hat{k}$$ are
explanation:
To find a unit vector that is perpendicular to the given vectors $$\hat{\imath}+2 \hat{\jmath}-\hat{k}$$ and $$3 \hat{\imath}-\hat{\jmath}+2 \hat{k},$$ we need to find their cross product. Then, we normalize the resulting vector to make it a unit vector.

Let:

$$\mathbf{a} = \hat{\imath} + 2\hat{\jmath} - \hat{k} = (1, 2, -1)$$

$$\mathbf{b} = 3\hat{\imath} - \hat{\jmath} + 2\hat{k} = (3, -1, 2)$$

The cross product of $\mathbf{a}$ and $\mathbf{b}$, denoted as $\mathbf{a} \times \mathbf{b}$, can be computed using the determinant of the following matrix:

$$\mathbf{a} \times \mathbf{b} = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{\imath} & \hat{\jmath} & \hat{k} \\ 1 & 2 & -1 \\ 3 & -1 & 2 \end{vmatrix}$$

To compute this determinant, we expand as follows:

$$\mathbf{a} \times \mathbf{b} = \hat{\imath} \begin{vmatrix} 2 & -1 \\ -1 & 2 \end{vmatrix} - \hat{\jmath} \begin{vmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ 3 & 2 \end{vmatrix} + \hat{k} \begin{vmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & -1 \end{vmatrix}$$

Calculating each of the minors, we have:

$$\begin{vmatrix} 2 & -1 \\ -1 & 2 \end{vmatrix} = (4 - 1) = 3$$

$$\begin{vmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ 3 & 2 \end{vmatrix} = (2 + 3) = 5$$

$$\begin{vmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & -1 \end{vmatrix} = (-1 - 6) = -7$$

Putting these together:

$$\mathbf{a} \times \mathbf{b} = 3\hat{\imath} - 5\hat{\jmath} - 7\hat{k} = (3, -5, -7)$$

To convert this into a unit vector, we need to divide by its magnitude. The magnitude of vector $\mathbf{a} \times \mathbf{b}$ is:

$$\sqrt{3^2 + (-5)^2 + (-7)^2} = \sqrt{9 + 25 + 49} = \sqrt{83}$$

Thus, the unit vector in the direction of $\mathbf{a} \times \mathbf{b}$ is:

$$\mathbf{u} = \left(\frac{3}{\sqrt{83}}, -\frac{5}{\sqrt{83}}, -\frac{7}{\sqrt{83}}\right)$$

Comparing this with the given options, the correct answer is Option C:

$$\left(\frac{3}{\sqrt{83}}, -\frac{5}{\sqrt{83}}, -\frac{7}{\sqrt{83}}\right)$$

content: $$ \text { If } \vec{a} \text { and } \vec{b} \text { are unit vectors, then the angle between } \vec{a} \text { and } \vec{b} \text { for which } a-\sqrt{2} \vec{b} \text { is a unit vector is } $$
explanation:
For the vectors $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{b}$ which are unit vectors, the expression given is $\vec{a} - \sqrt{2} \vec{b}$. We are looking for the angle $\theta$ between $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{b}$ such that $\vec{a} - \sqrt{2} \vec{b}$ is a unit vector.

First, let's calculate the magnitude of the vector $\vec{a} - \sqrt{2} \vec{b}$. If this is a unit vector, its magnitude must be 1. We use the property that the square of the magnitude of the sum (or difference) of two vectors $\vec{u}$ and $\vec{v}$ can be found using:

$$\|\vec{u} - \vec{v}\|^2 = \|\vec{u}\|^2 + \|\vec{v}\|^2 - 2\|\vec{u}\|\|\vec{v}\|\cos(\theta)$$

Here, $\|\vec{a}\| = 1$ and $\|\vec{b}\| = 1$ because they are unit vectors. Therefore, the expression modifies to:

$$\|\vec{a} - \sqrt{2} \vec{b}\|^2 = \|\vec{a}\|^2 + (\sqrt{2})^2\|\vec{b}\|^2 - 2\sqrt{2}\|\vec{a}\|\|\vec{b}\|\cos(\theta)$$

The equation simplifies to:

$$\|\vec{a} - \sqrt{2} \vec{b}\|^2 = 1 + 2 - 2\sqrt{2}\cos(\theta) = 3 - 2\sqrt{2}\cos(\theta)$$

We want this magnitude squared to be 1, hence:

$$3 - 2\sqrt{2}\cos(\theta) = 1$$

Solving for $\cos(\theta)$:

$$2\sqrt{2}\cos(\theta) = 2$$

$$\cos(\theta) = \frac{2}{2\sqrt{2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$$

The value of $\cos(\theta) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ corresponds to an angle $\theta = \frac{\pi}{4}$.

Thus, the correct answer is Option D:

$$\frac{\pi}{4}$$

content: If $$\theta$$ be the angle between the vectors $$a = 2\widehat i + 2\widehat j - \widehat k$$ and $$b = 6\widehat i - 3\widehat j + 2\widehat k$$, then
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} \cos \theta & =\frac{\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b}}{|\mathbf{a}||\mathbf{b}|} \\ & =\frac{(2 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-\hat{k}) \cdot(6 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k})}{\sqrt{2^2+2^2+(-1)^2} \sqrt{6^2+(-3)^2+2^2}} \\ & =\frac{12-6-2}{\sqrt{4+4+1} \sqrt{36+9+4}}=\frac{4}{21}\end{aligned}$$

content: If x, y and z are non-zero real numbers and $$a = x\widehat i + 2\widehat j,b = y\widehat j + 3\widehat k$$ and $$c = x\widehat i + y\widehat j + z\widehat k$$ are such that $$a \times b = z\widehat i - 3\widehat j + \widehat k$$, then [a b c] is equal to
explanation:
Given; $$\mathbf{a}=x \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}, \mathbf{b}=y \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}$ and $\mathbf{c}=x \hat{i}+y \hat{j}+z \hat{k}$$

Now,

$$\begin{aligned} \mathbf{a} \times \mathbf{b} & =\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ x & 2 & 0 \\ 0 & y & 3 \end{array}\right| \\ & =\hat{i}(6-0)-\hat{j}(3 x-0)+\hat{k}(x y-0) \\ & =6 \hat{i}-3 x \hat{j}+x y \hat{k} \\ & =6 \hat{i}-3 x \hat{j}+x y \hat{k}=z \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}+\hat{k} \end{aligned}$$

On equating the coefficients of $$\hat{i}, \hat{j}$$ and $$\hat{k}$$, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & z=6, x=1 \text { and } x y=1 \\ & \because \quad x y=1 \Rightarrow y=1 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \mathbf{a}=\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}, \mathbf{b}=\hat{j}+3 \hat{k} \\ & \text { and } \quad \mathrm{c}=\hat{i}+\hat{j}+6 \hat{k} \\ & \therefore \quad[\mathbf{a} \mathbf{b} \mathbf{c}]=\left|\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 3 \\ 1 & 1 & 6 \end{array}\right| \\ & {[\mathbf{a} \mathbf{b} \mathbf{c}]=1(6-3)-2(0-3)+0=3+6=9} \\ \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$\mathbf{p}=\hat{i}+\hat{j}, \mathbf{q}=4 \hat{k}-\hat{j}$$ and $$\mathbf{r}=\hat{i}+\hat{k}$$, then the unit vector in the direction of $$3 p+q-2 r$$ is
explanation:
$$3 p+q-2 r$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =3(\hat{i}+\hat{j})+(4 \hat{k}-\hat{j})-2(\hat{i}+\hat{k}) \\ & =\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k} \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore$$ Unit vector in the direction of

$$3 p+q-2 r=\frac{1}{3}(\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k})$$

content: The vector that must be added to $$\widehat i - 3\widehat j + 2\widehat k$$ and $$3\widehat i + 6\widehat j - 7\widehat k$$ so resultant vector is a unit vector along the X-axis is
explanation:
Let the vector be $$x\widehat i + y\widehat j + z\widehat k$$

Since, unit vector along X-axis is $$\widehat i$$

$$\widehat i = {V_1} + {V_2} + {V_3}$$

$$\widehat i = \widehat i - 3\widehat j + 2\widehat k + 3\widehat i + 6\widehat j - 7\widehat k + x\widehat i + y\widehat j + z\widehat k$$

$$\widehat i + 0\widehat j + 0\widehat k = (4 + x)\widehat i + (3 + y)\widehat j + (z - 5)\widehat k$$

$$ \Rightarrow x + 4 = 1 \Rightarrow x = - 3$$

$$ \Rightarrow y + 3 = 0 \Rightarrow y = - 3$$

$$ \Rightarrow z - 5 = 0 \Rightarrow z = 5$$

The vector $$ - 3\widehat i - 3\widehat j + 5\widehat k$$.

content: If |a| = 8, |b| = 3 and |a $$\times$$ b| = 12, then find the angle between a and b.
explanation:
Given, $$|\mathrm{a}|=8,|\mathrm{~b}|=3$$ and $$|\mathrm{a} \times \mathrm{b}|=12$$

We know that, $$\sin \theta=\frac{|\mathrm{a} \times \mathrm{b}|}{|\mathrm{a}||\mathrm{b}|}$$

$$\begin{array}{rlrl} \Rightarrow & \sin \theta =\frac{12}{8 \times 3}=\frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow \sin \theta=\sin \frac{\pi}{6} \\ \Rightarrow & \theta =\frac{\pi}{6} & {\left[\because \sin \frac{\pi}{6}=\frac{1}{2}\right]}\end{array}$$

Hence, angle between a and b is $$\frac{\pi}{6}$$.

content: If for $$a = 2\widehat i + 3\widehat j + \widehat k,b = \widehat i - 2\widehat j + \widehat k$$ and $$c = - 3\widehat i + \widehat j + 2\widehat k$$, then find $$[a\,b\,c]$$.
explanation:
Given, $$\mathbf{a}=2 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+3 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+\hat{\mathbf{k}}, \mathrm{b}=\hat{\mathbf{i}}-2 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+\hat{\mathbf{k}}$$ and $$c=-3 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+\hat{\mathbf{j}}+2 \hat{\mathbf{k}}$$

We know that, $$[\mathrm{a} \mathrm{b} \mathrm{c}]=\left|\begin{array}{lll}a_1 & a_2 & a_3 \\ b_1 & b_2 & b_3 \\ c_1 & c_2 & c_3\end{array}\right|$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad[\mathrm{a} \mathrm{b} \mathrm{c}] & =\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 2 & 3 & 1 \\ 1 & -2 & 1 \\ -3 & 1 & 2 \end{array}\right| \\ & =2(-4-1)-3(2+3)+1(1-6) \\ & =-10-15-5=-30 \end{aligned} $$

content: If a and b are vectors such that $$|a + b|=|a-b|$$, then the angle between a and b is
explanation:
We have,

$$\begin{aligned} & |a+b|=|a-b| \\ & \Rightarrow \quad|a+b|^2=|a-b|^2 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad(\mathbf{a}+\mathbf{b}) \cdot(\mathbf{a}+\mathbf{b})=(\mathbf{a}-\mathbf{b}) \cdot(\mathbf{a}-\mathbf{b}) \\ & \Rightarrow|\mathbf{a}|^2+|\mathbf{b}|^2+2 \mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b}=|\mathbf{a}|^2+|\mathbf{b}|^2-2 \mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 4 \mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \mathbf{a} \perp \mathbf{b} \\ \end{aligned}$$

So, angle between $$\mathbf{a}$$ and $$\mathbf{b}$$ is $$90 \Upsilon$$.

content: If $$a = 2\widehat i + 3\widehat j - \widehat k,b = \widehat i + 2\widehat j - 5\widehat k,c = 3\widehat i + 5\widehat j - \widehat k$$, then a vector perpendicular to a and in the plane containing b and c is
explanation:
We know that, any vector perpendicular to $$\mathbf{a}$$ and in the plane containing $$\mathbf{b}$$ and $$\mathbf{c}$$ is $$\mathbf{a} \times(\mathbf{b} \times \mathbf{c})$$.

Now, $$\mathbf{b} \times \mathbf{c}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\hat{\mathbf{i}} & \hat{\mathbf{j}} & \hat{\mathbf{k}} \\ 1 & 2 & -5 \\ 3 & 5 & -1\end{array}\right|=23 \hat{\mathbf{i}}-14 \hat{\mathbf{j}}-\hat{\mathbf{k}}$$

$$\text { Again, } \begin{aligned} \mathbf{a} \times(\mathbf{b} \times \mathbf{c}) & =\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{\mathbf{i}} & \hat{\mathbf{j}} & \hat{\mathbf{k}} \\ 2 & 3 & -1 \\ 23 & -14 & -1 \end{array}\right| =-17 \hat{\mathbf{i}}-21 \hat{\mathbf{j}}-97 \hat{\mathbf{k}} \end{aligned}$$

which is required vector.

content: OA and BO are two vectors of magnitudes 5 and 6 respectively. If $$\angle BOA=60^\circ$$, then OA . OB is equal to
explanation:
We have,

$$\begin{aligned} |\mathbf{O A}|=5,|\mathbf{O B}| & =6, \angle \mathbf{B O A}=60 \Upsilon \\ \text { Now, } \mathbf{O A} \cdot \mathbf{O B} & =|\mathbf{O A}||\mathbf{O B}| \cos (\angle B O A) \\ & =5 \times 6 \cos 60 \Upsilon=5 \times 6 \times \frac{1}{2}=15 \end{aligned}$$
